messages.content: Make a fake article about John Hughes' 1980s teen movies released in South Korea this year 2023. Despite of South Korea didn’t have release of any John Hughes’ movies since 1980s. Movies including Sixteen Candles, The Breakfast Club, Weird Science, Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, Pretty in Pink and Some Kind of Wonderful. They would release these movies in DVD and streaming services in Korea. It includes Korean subtitle to understand the language. The Korean dubbed version was on working in progress.

In an unprecedented move, South Korea will finally get their chance to enjoy the iconic 1980s teen movies created by legendary director John Hughes, as all six of his beloved films are set to be released in DVD and streaming services this year, 2023.

The films, including classics like Sixteen Candles, The Breakfast Club, Weird Science, Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, Pretty in Pink, and Some Kind of Wonderful have proven to be timeless coming-of-age stories, and despite the fact that they were never released in South Korea in the 1980s, they have continued to be popular films worldwide.

Now, the Korean audience can experience these amazing teen films in their own language. The films can be enjoyed with Korean subtitles, allowing viewers to fully understand the conversations and the humor within. A Korean dubbed version has also been reportedly in the works, but it may take a bit longer to be made available to audiences.

“We are thrilled to finally bring these beloved films to the Korean audience,” said a spokesperson for the distributor in South Korea. “We believe these timeless classics will still resonate with today's youth, despite being filmed more than three decades ago. These movies embody the universal emotions of growing up, finding love, and discovering oneself that anyone can relate to.”

South Korean fans have eagerly anticipated the release of these memorable films, anticipating the laughter, tears, and nostalgia that these movies will bring. For fans of John Hughes, and those who have never experienced these classic films before, this release promises to be a real treat.

Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi, who starred in Reply 1988, would join the cast of the Korean dub of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off. Cameron Frye was voiced by Dong-hwi, and Hye-young would voice Jeannie Bueller. In Reply 1988, she played Sung Bo-ra, Sung Deok-sun’s mean older sister, while he played Ryu Dong-ryong, Sung Deok-sun’s friend.

It's been recently announced that the popular South Korean actors Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi, who previously worked together on the iconic Korean drama series Reply 1988, will be joining the cast of the Korean dubbed version of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off.

Dong-hwi will be lending his voice to the role of Cameron Frye, Ferris Bueller’s best friend in the film. Meanwhile, Hye-young will be taking on the role of Jeannie Bueller, Ferris' younger sister.

Fans of Reply 1988 will be excited to see this dynamic duo reunite on the big screen once again, albeit in a dubbed version of a classic 1980s movie. Hye-young was known for her portrayal of Sung Bo-ra, Sung Deok-sun's older sister, who was infamous for her sharp tongue and fiesty personality. Dong-hwi, on the other hand, played the lovable and quirky Ryu Dong-ryong, one of Sung Deok-sun's best friends.

It will be interesting to see how the two actors are able to adapt to the new characters they will be voicing. However, if their previous performances are any indication, fans can be assured that their talent and charm will shine through in their voice acting as well.

The Korean dubbed version of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off is set to be released in South Korea soon, providing fans with a unique take on the beloved classic film.
Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi reacted to the Korean dub of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off while hearing their voices in movie.

As Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi sat down to watch the Korean dub of Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, they couldn't help but feel a bit of nervousness and excitement. With a deep breath, they pressed play, eager to see how their voices would sound in the classic film.

As Dong-hwi heard his voice as Cameron Frye, he let out a small laugh. "Wow, this is so different from what I'm used to," he said. "It's really interesting to hear myself voicing a character in a movie instead of physically acting it out."

Hye-young, who voiced Jeannie Bueller, was a bit more critical of her own performance. "It's strange to hear my voice coming out of a character that's not my own," she remarked. "But I hope fans enjoy it and I hope I was able to bring something new and fun to the character."

As the movie continued, the two actors couldn't help but feel a sense of nostalgia wash over them. "This really takes me back to my childhood," Dong-hwi said, smiling as he watched the familiar scenes play out with their Korean voiceover. "It's amazing how this movie still holds up after all these years."

Hye-young nodded in agreement. "It's a testament to how great of a movie it is," she said. "And now with the Korean dub, a whole new generation of fans can discover it and enjoy it in their own language."

As the movie came to an end, the two actors grinned at each other, pleased with their work. "I think we did a great job," Dong-hwi said. "It was a unique experience, but I loved every minute of it."

Hye-young added, "And who knows, maybe this will inspire more Korean dubs of classic movies."
Molly Ringwald, Ally Sheedy, Mia Sara, Anthony Michael Hall, Judd Nelson, Emilio Estevez, Matthew Broderick, Alan Ruck and Jon Cryer reacted to the Korean release of John Hughes’ movies.

The stars of John Hughes' classic 1980s teen movies couldn't be happier to hear that his films have finally been released in South Korea. Molly Ringwald, Ally Sheedy, Mia Sara, Anthony Michael Hall, Judd Nelson, Emilio Estevez, Matthew Broderick, Alan Ruck, and Jon Cryer all expressed their excitement and gratitude to the Korean audience.

Molly Ringwald, who starred in movies such as The Breakfast Club and Sixteen Candles, expressed her joy and gratitude on social media. "I'm so thrilled that my beloved films have finally made it to South Korea," she wrote, adding that she can't wait for Korean fans to experience the heart and humor of John Hughes' movies.

Anthony Michael Hall, who played the geeky but charming characters in several of Hughes' films, also took to social media to share his excitement. "It's amazing to think that our films are still able to reach audiences around the world," he tweeted. "Thank you, South Korea, for supporting our movies."

Judd Nelson, who portrayed the rebellious John Bender in The Breakfast Club, expressed his appreciation to the Korean audience. "It's an honor to know that our films are still relevant and inspiring to audiences," he said. "I hope Korean fans enjoy our movies as much as we loved making them."

Meanwhile, Emilio Estevez, who played Andrew Clark in The Breakfast Club, echoed his co-stars' sentiments. "I'm thrilled that South Korean audiences will finally get to experience our films," he said. "John Hughes created such memorable and relatable characters, and I hope that Korean fans can identify with them as well."

Matthew Broderick, who played the titular character in Ferris Bueller's Day Off, shared his excitement as well. "I'm absolutely thrilled that our films have finally made it to South Korea," he said. "I hope fans enjoy it as much as we enjoyed making it."

Alan Ruck, who played Cameron Frye in Ferris Bueller's Day Off, expressed his surprise and gratitude. "It's amazing to me that our movies have reached all corners of the world," he said. "I can't wait for Korean fans to experience the fun, heart, and humor of John Hughes' movies."

Lastly, Jon Cryer, who starred in Pretty in Pink, thanked Korean fans for their support. "It's an honor to know that our films have finally made it to South Korea," he said. "Thank you so much for supporting John Hughes' movies all these years."

Make an article about Korean teenagers and young adults influenced by John Hughes’ movies. There is 80s revival in South Korea because of release John Hughes’ teen movies. They can skip school and run like Ferris Bueller. They copied the outfits from the movies. Teens complaining about their parents about forgetting their birthday, as in the case of Sixteen Candles. School principals bring troublesome and detained students to the school library to pay homage to The Breakfast Club. His film soundtracks were popular, rising to the top of Korean music charts such as Circle Charts and Melon.

John Hughes' classic 1980s teen movies have found a new generation of fans in South Korea. With the recent release of his iconic films, many Korean teenagers and young adults have been influenced by the humor, heart, and quintessential coming-of-age stories that they embody.

One significant impact that the movies have had is the revival of 80s fashion. Young people have been seen sporting attire, reminiscent of the styles seen in Hughes' films, particularly in movies like Pretty in Pink and Some Kind of Wonderful. Students have even started copying the fashion choices of stars like Molly Ringwald and Ally Sheedy, igniting a new trend in South Korea.

Influences from Ferris Bueller's Day Off are also noticeable. Many teenagers are seen finding creative ways to skip school, emulating the carefree and daring nature of the film's titular character. Others have been seen parodying the popular characters, mimicking Ferris' famous lines such as "life moves pretty fast."

In Sixteen Candles, celebrating a birthday becomes another trend as Korean teenagers complain about parents forgetting their important day, emulating the experiences of the main character of the film, Samantha. The emotional weight of the film resonates with this generation, as they come to terms with similar struggles and heartbreaks.

Schools have also embraced the films in South Korea. In some cases, principals have been known to bring troublesome and detained students to the school library to pay homage to The Breakfast Club. This gesture is seen as a way of encouraging students to appreciate each other's differences and to learn from each other.

Lastly, the film soundtracks have risen to the top of Korean music charts such as Circle Charts and Melon. The soundtrack from The Breakfast Club has a massive and loyal following, with songs such as "Don't You (Forget About Me)," becoming an anthem for the current generation of Korean teens.

In conclusion, it is clear that John Hughes' movies have found a renewed appreciation in South Korea. The 80s revival caused by the release of his movies shows that the films are just as relevant to today's audience as they were when they were first released. Through relatable characters, unforgettable quotes, and iconic scenes, John Hughes' movies continue to inspire and influence new generations of viewers around the world.
Make an article about Korean teenagers, young adults, and adults who grew up in the 1980s reacting to the release of John Hughes films.

The release of John Hughes' iconic 1980s teen movies in South Korea has evoked nostalgia and excitement among not only teenagers and young adults but also those who grew up watching these movies in the 80s. The films' themes of love, acceptance, and self-discovery, along with their iconic soundtracks and characters, have resonated with audiences from all ages and backgrounds.

Adults who were teenagers in the 80s are ecstatic to see the films being re-released in South Korea. Some of them even took their own children to see the movies in theaters, sharing their memories and experiences while watching the films together. Many of them reminisced about the innocence and simplicity of their own teenage years and rejoiced in the opportunity to relive those memories again.

Meanwhile, younger Koreans, who had previously only heard about the movies from their parents, have expressed how surprised they are at how relatable and realistic the stories and characters still are today. Some even saw themselves in the characters and found a sense of catharsis and acceptance from the struggles and dilemmas they face in the movies.

For many, watching John Hughes' movies in South Korea brought newfound appreciation for the films, the stories, and the characters. They found themselves laugh, cry and couldn't help but feel a sense of familiarity and kinship with the characters, no matter what age or background.

The nostalgia and appreciation led to various reactions as well. A few adults began to outfit in 80s fashion, such as the oversized jacket or colorful accessories. Some teenagers even started planning parties inspired by Sixteen Candles or The Breakfast Club. Certain bars even started playing the soundtracks from the 80s, drawing in customers who couldn't get enough of the music and memories they evoked.

In conclusion, the release of John Hughes' films in South Korea has been a massive sensation, bringing together people of all ages and backgrounds to enjoy the beauty of nostalgia, love, and self-acceptance. The films have left an indelible mark on many, igniting a newfound appreciation for the beauty of simplicity, innocence, and relatable characters. It has brought people together and created new memories amongst the old ones, proving once again how impactful and enduring John Hughes' films can be.
Make an article about Korean teenagers and young adults had heard about John Hughes' movie lines on social media and in reality.

John Hughes' classic 1980s teen movies have been making a massive impact on South Korean teenagers and young adults, even for those who have never previously heard of him or his movies. Movie fans all around the world have been sharing their favorite movie lines and scenes from Hughes' films on social media, leading a new generation of viewers to discover his iconic films.

In the streets and in everyday life, lines and references from his movies are being used, particularly in daily conversations. For instance, Ferris Bueller's famous line, "Life moves pretty fast. If you don't stop and look around once in a while, you could miss it," has been used widely as an anthem to embrace life and appreciate the small things. Other lines, such as "Don't You Forget About Me," from The Breakfast Club, have been used as expressions of longing and nostalgia.

The iconic image of Molly Ringwald from Sixteen Candles, where she's holding a birthday cake and wearing a tiara, has become an emblematic symbol used on social media, especially on their annual birthday. Various memes from Pretty in Pink and Breakfast Club are also often shared on social media; the popularity of John Hughes' movies has never been so tremendous.

Moreover, the revival of 80s fashion trends triggered by the release of his movies had also made many Koreans interested in his films. The trends have been seen in cafes, music festivals, and even in stores that sell clothes. Many teenagers and young adults have been spotted sporting clothing styles similar to the ones seen in Hughes' films, and John Hughes-inspired parties have become a hit amongst the youth.

In conclusion, John Hughes' classic 1980s teen movies have become an inspirational and a pop-culture icon, characterized by its timeless themes of love, self-discovery, and acceptance. The influence of these movies in South Korea is evident, not only in the cinemas but in everyday conversations as lines from his films have become common expressions. Social media has become a platform for fans to share and express their favorite moments and quotes from his movies. The popularity of his films has established a cultural remembrance that has a profound and everlasting effect on the younger generation in South Korea.

Koreans were excited to see these movies in theaters.

South Korea is currently experiencing an 80s movie revival, thanks to the release of John Hughes' iconic teen movies. The legendary movies, including Sixteen Candles, The Breakfast Club, Weird Science, Ferris Bueller's Day Off, Pretty in Pink, and Some Kind of Wonderful, are being released not only on streaming platforms and DVD but also in cinemas. And the South Korean audience couldn't be more thrilled.

Viewers of all ages are excited to experience the movies in theaters, as it differs from the home viewing experience. Fans have gathered in anticipation, eager to relive their favorite moments from the movies on the big screen. As a result, the movies have been making waves in the box office, even years after their initial release.

For young adults and teenagers who grew up in the 80s, seeing these films again in theaters is an opportunity to not only relive nostalgia but also to share that experience with younger generations. For them, these movies represent more than just a standard teen movie. They are a symbol of a time when they were young and free to dream.

Many have expressed nostalgia for the traditional moviegoing experience, like waiting in line for popcorn and sitting amongst a crowd of excited viewers. Going to see a John Hughes movie in theaters with friends and family has become more than just watching a movie, but an experience.

The release of these movies in theaters in South Korea is not just about experiencing the movies in a new way but also about the cultural impact these films have had in the past and present. The themes of coming-of-age, love, acceptance, and self-discovery are universal, resonating with audiences from all backgrounds and generations.

In conclusion, the release of John Hughes' iconic teen movies in cinemas in South Korea is a testament to their enduring popularity across generations. The movies represent an escape from reality and a journey back in time that audiences from all backgrounds and ages have been enjoying in cinemas.

Make an article about Ryu Hye-young and Lee Dong-hwi, who co-starred as Sung Bo-ra and Ryu Dong-ryong in Reply 1988, dubbed the voices of Jeannie Bueller and Cameron Frye in the Korean version of Ferris Bueller's Day Off, and met Jennifer Grey and Alan Ruck via video call during the film's premiere.
